core_concept.explanation: sp² hybridization occurs when one $s$ orbital mixes with two $p$ orbitals to form three equivalent $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals. These orbitals arrange themselves in a trigonal planar geometry with $120^\circ$ bond angles. The one remaining unhybridized $p$ orbital is perpendicular to the hybrid plane and enables $\pi$ bond formation, creating double bonds characteristic of alkenes.

image.alt: Diagram showing one s and two p orbitals mixing to form three sp² orbitals
image.caption: Mixing one s and two p orbitals yields three sp² hybrids and one unhybridized p orbital

sections.introduction: sp² hybridization is observed in atoms with a steric number of 3, most commonly in alkenes where carbon forms a double bond. This hybridization state provides 33% s-character, resulting in bonds that are intermediate in strength and length between sp³ and sp hybridized systems.

introduction_image.alt: Trigonal planar geometry showing 120 degree bond angles
introduction_image.caption: sp² hybrid orbitals orient in a plane at 120 degree angles

key_concept.definition: sp² hybrid orbitals are formed by the linear combination of one s and two p atomic orbitals, resulting in three coplanar hybrids with one perpendicular unhybridized p orbital.
key_concept.context: Fundamental to alkene chemistry, carbonyl groups, and aromatic systems where planar geometry and π bonding are essential.

image.alt: Shape of sp² hybrid orbitals showing trigonal planar arrangement
image.caption: Three sp² orbitals lie in a plane; the unhybridized p orbital is perpendicular

principles.application: The single unhybridized $p$ orbital is available for $\pi$ bonding, enabling double bond formation while maintaining planar geometry.
principles.analogy: Like a tripod (three sp² legs) standing on a flat surface, with a flagpole (p orbital) sticking straight up from the center.

image.alt: Diagram showing orbital count conservation in sp² hybridization
image.caption: Total orbitals before and after hybridization remain constant (4)

principles.statement: sp² hybrids have 33% s-character, between sp³ (25%) and sp (50%).
principles.application: This intermediate s-character produces bond lengths and strengths between single and triple bonds, and moderate acidity for attached hydrogens.
principles.example: Ethene C=C bond length (134 pm) is shorter than ethane C-C (154 pm) but longer than ethyne C≡C (120 pm).

image.alt: Comparison of bond lengths for sp³, sp², and sp hybridized carbons
image.caption: Bond length decreases as s-character increases

principles.statement: The trigonal planar arrangement and π bond formation lock sp² centers into a rigid plane.
principles.application: Rotation around C=C double bonds is restricted, enabling geometric (cis/trans) isomerism in alkenes.
principles.example: cis-2-butene and trans-2-butene are distinct compounds with different physical properties due to restricted rotation.

image.alt: Cis and trans isomers of 2-butene showing planar geometry
image.caption: Planar sp² geometry enables geometric isomerism

components.definition: The sp² hybrid wavefunction combines s and two p orbitals: $\psi_{sp^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\phi_s + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\phi_p$
components.interpretation: The 33% s-character provides moderate electron density near the nucleus, influencing electronegativity and reactivity of sp² carbons.

image.alt: Mathematical representation of sp² hybrid wavefunction
image.caption: Unequal contribution from s and p orbitals defines sp² hybrids

classifications.value: Steric Number = 3 (3 sigma bonds, 0 lone pairs OR 2 sigma bonds + 1 lone pair)
characteristics: Contains double bonds or lone pairs in plane
classifications.behavior: Forms one sigma bond framework with one pi bond capability via unhybridized p orbital

examples.explanation: Each carbon forms 3 sigma bonds (2 C-H, 1 C-C). The unhybridized p orbitals overlap to form one pi bond.

image.alt: Structure of ethene showing trigonal planar geometry
image.caption: Ethene is the simplest alkene with sp² carbons

examples.explanation: Carbon in C=O is sp² hybridized; oxygen also uses sp² hybrids with lone pairs in the plane.
examples.value: Planar structure enables nucleophilic attack perpendicular to the plane

examples.explanation: All six carbons are sp² hybridized, creating a planar ring with delocalized π system.

image.alt: Benzene ring showing sp² hybridization and delocalized pi system
image.caption: Aromatic systems rely on continuous sp² hybridization

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Bonding in Propene (C₃H₆)
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of each carbon in propene (CH₃-CH=CH₂), describe the orbital overlap in the C=C double bond, and predict the geometry around each carbon.

step_by_step.explanation: Propene has a methyl group attached to an ethene unit: CH₃-CH=CH₂. Identify bonding patterns for each carbon.
step_by_step.calculation: C₁ (methyl): 4 single bonds; C₂ (alkene): 1 single + 1 double; C₃ (alkene): 1 single + 1 double

step_by_step.explanation: C₁: bonded to 3H + 1C = 4 domains → SN=4. C₂ & C₃: each bonded to 3 atoms (counting double bond as 1 domain) → SN=3.
step_by_step.calculation: C₁: SN=4 → sp³; C₂,C₃: SN=3 → sp²

step_by_step.explanation: C₁ (sp³): tetrahedral, 109.5°. C₂,C₃ (sp²): trigonal planar, ~120°. Unhybridized p orbitals on C₂,C₃ form π bond.
The sp² carbons and attached atoms lie in one plane; the methyl group can rotate freely around the C₁-C₂ single bond.
step_by_step.calculation: Planar region: C₂, C₃, and their 4 attached H atoms; Rotatable: CH₃ group

worked_examples.final_answer: C₁ is sp³ (tetrahedral); C₂ and C₃ are sp² (trigonal planar). The C=C double bond consists of one sigma and one pi bond. The alkene portion is planar, enabling potential cis/trans isomerism in substituted derivatives.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number determines hybridization; sp² hybridization enables planar geometry and pi bonding.

image.alt: Orbital overlap diagram for propene showing mixed hybridization
image.caption: Propene combines sp³ and sp² hybridized carbons in one molecule

process_flow.process: Identify Double Bond or Trigonal Center
process_flow.description: Look for C=C, C=O, C=N, or atoms with three bonded groups and no lone pairs (or two bonds + one lone pair).
process_flow.mechanism: Double bonds require one pi bond, necessitating one unhybridized p orbital perpendicular to the sigma framework.
process_flow.result: Candidate atoms identified for sp² hybridization

image.alt: Examples of molecules with sp² centers
image.caption: Identify atoms involved in double bonds or trigonal arrangements

process_flow.description: Confirm the atom has exactly 3 electron domains (bonded atoms + lone pairs).

image.alt: Steric number calculation for sp² atom
image.caption: Steric number of 3 confirms sp² hybridization

process_flow.description: Orient the unhybridized p orbital perpendicular to the plane of the three sp² hybrids.
process_flow.mechanism: Sideways overlap of parallel p orbitals on adjacent atoms forms the pi bond
process_flow.result: Electron density above and below the molecular plane

image.alt: Perpendicular pi orbital in sp² hybridized system
image.caption: The pi bond creates electron density above and below the planar sigma framework

sections.role: Foundation of Alkene Chemistry and Planar Systems

components.function: Form three sigma bonds in a trigonal planar arrangement
components.key_feature: 120° bond angles optimize orbital separation and minimize repulsion

image.alt: Detailed view of sp² hybrid orbital arrangement
image.caption: Three sp² orbitals lie in a plane with 120 degree separation

components.function: Forms one pi bond via sideways overlap with adjacent p orbital
components.key_feature: Perpendicular orientation ($90^\circ$ to hybrid plane) enables pi bonding

image.alt: Unhybridized p orbital perpendicular to sp² plane
image.caption: The p orbital is orthogonal to the plane of the sp² hybrids

function.operation: sp² hybrids form the planar sigma framework; the p orbital creates the reactive pi bond above and below the plane.
function.energy: sp² C-H bonds are stronger than sp³ but weaker than sp; pi bonds are weaker than sigma bonds and more reactive.
function.mechanism: Planar geometry exposes pi electrons for electrophilic attack; restricted rotation enables stereoisomerism.

image.alt: Function diagram showing sigma framework and pi reactivity in alkenes
image.caption: Sigma bonds provide planar structure; pi bonds provide site of electrophilic addition

cycle.operation: Head-on overlap of sp² hybrids with partner atoms (C or H)
cycle.purpose: Establishes rigid trigonal planar molecular skeleton

image.alt: Sigma bond formation in sp² system
image.caption: sp² hybrids form the planar sigma backbone

cycle.operation: Sideways overlap of parallel unhybridized p orbitals
cycle.purpose: Creates double bond character and electron-rich region for reactivity

image.alt: Pi bond formation in sp² system
image.caption: The pi bond creates electron density above and below the molecular plane

cycle.operation: Electrophiles attack the exposed pi electron cloud
cycle.purpose: Enables addition reactions characteristic of alkenes

image.alt: Electrophilic addition to alkene pi bond
image.caption: Pi electrons are accessible for electrophilic addition reactions

categories.description: Restricted rotation around C=C double bonds allows for cis/trans (E/Z) stereoisomers when each carbon has two different substituents.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The pi bond locks the two carbons and their four attached groups into a single plane; rotation would require breaking the pi bond (~270 kJ/mol).

examples.explanation: Methyl groups on same side of double bond; higher boiling point due to molecular dipole.

image.alt: Structure of cis-2-butene
image.caption: Cis isomer has substituents on same side

examples.explanation: Methyl groups on opposite sides; lower energy due to reduced steric strain.

image.alt: Structure of trans-2-butene
image.caption: Trans isomer has substituents on opposite sides

image.alt: Comparison of cis and trans isomers
image.caption: Geometric isomers have different physical and chemical properties

summary: sp² hybridization involves mixing one $s$ and two $p$ orbitals to create three trigonal planar $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals oriented $120^\circ$ apart. This leaves one unhybridized $p$ orbital perpendicular to the hybrid plane, enabling formation of one $\pi$ bond alongside two $\sigma$ bonds (a double bond). sp²-hybridized carbons exhibit 33% s-character, producing bond properties intermediate between sp³ and sp systems. The planar geometry and restricted rotation around C=C double bonds enable geometric (cis/trans) isomerism, a cornerstone of alkene stereochemistry. Understanding sp² hybridization is essential for predicting alkene structure, stability, reactivity patterns (especially electrophilic addition), and spectroscopic properties.
